claim: The Voynich text uses a homophonic substitution cipher on medieval Latin where 25 glyphs encode approximately 18-20 Latin phonemes, with 2-4 glyphs mapping to each high-frequency Latin phoneme (e, a, i, o, t, n), which would explain the observed entropy of 3.8627 bits â€” lower than plain Latin (~4.0) but higher than a monoalphabetic cipher.
evidence_for: Glyph entropy 3.8627 bits falls between monoalphabetic cipher entropy (~3.6) and plain Latin entropy (~4.0), consistent with mild homophony
Only 25 unique glyphs encoding a ~23-letter Latin alphabet requires some glyphs to double-map
Word-final glyphs y, n, l, r, o overlap with common Latin word endings (-us, -um, -is, -nt, -or)
Multiple high-confidence beliefs (0.664, 0.645) in current state support homophonic substitution on Latin/Italian
evidence_against: Hapax ratio of 70.1% is far higher than expected for Latin text under homophonic substitution (~15-25%)
Zipf exponent 0.8946 is lower than typical natural language (~1.0), suggesting non-standard word frequency distribution
Word entropy 10.4508 bits is very high for Latin with only 25-glyph alphabet
test: Map each of the 25 glyphs to a candidate Latin phoneme using frequency matching (most frequent Voynich glyph â†’ most frequent Latin phoneme). Compute the bigram entropy of the resulting mapped text and compare to a corpus of medieval Latin bigram entropy. Also compute the frequency of mapped word endings against known Latin inflectional suffixes (-us, -um, -is, -ae, -orum).
test_metric: Bigram entropy of phoneme-mapped text; percentage of word endings matching top 10 Latin inflectional suffixes
test_threshold: Confirm if mapped bigram entropy falls within 0.2 bits of medieval Latin (~3.8 bits) AND >40% of word endings match Latin inflectional suffixes; deny if both metrics fall outside these ranges

reasoning: Homophonic substitution on Latin is the current highest-weighted cipher hypothesis. A precise mapping test would either strongly confirm or eliminate this class of cipher, narrowing the solution space significantly.
